title: Encoding High-Cardinality String Categorical Variables
authors: Patricio Cerda, Gaël Varoquaux (Inria, PARIETAL / NEUROSPIN)
venue: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering (TKDE) 2020; arXiv:1907.01860 (submitted 2019). Methods shipped as dirty_cat MinHashEncoder / GapEncoder, now skrub.
tokenization_method: Substring/n-gram-based encoders that map a raw string category to a fixed d-dimensional dense vector WITHOUT a fixed vocabulary. Two main proposals: (1) MinHash encoder — represent each string as the set of its character n-grams, apply d independent hash functions, keep the per-function minimum hash; the resulting d-vector is a locality-sensitive signature whose collision rate equals the Jaccard similarity of the n-gram sets. (2) Gamma-Poisson factorization (GapEncoder) — build a (categories x n-grams) count matrix and factorize it into d non-negative latent 'topics' under a Gamma prior on activations and Poisson likelihood on counts, giving interpretable soft membership of each string in d latent substring-categories. A 3-gram similarity encoding (tf-idf string-similarity to prototype categories) is the prior-art baseline they extend.
feature_types_handled: categorical only — specifically high-cardinality, dirty/free-text string categoricals (typos, morphological variants, compound job titles, addresses). No numerical, temporal, or missing-value mechanism; these encoders sit on string columns and emit dense vectors that a downstream model concatenates with other features.
token_granularity: per-feature dense code: one string value -> one d-dimensional vector (default d=30). It is a value-level encoder (each distinct string gets its own vector) but the output width is per-column-fixed and decoupled from cardinality, so it behaves like a fixed-width per-field token at the column level. Sub-structure is at the character-n-gram level, not whole-word.
numerical_strategy: none (out of scope for this work; numerical columns are left to the downstream pipeline).
categorical_strategy: Two stateless/online dense encoders over character n-grams: MinHash (stateless LSH signature, no fitting) and Gamma-Poisson topic factorization (fit by online/streaming EM-style updates). Both replace one-hot/target/similarity encoding for messy strings. Output feeds gradient-boosted trees or any tabular learner.
high_cardinality_handling: This is the entire point. One-hot is O(cardinality)-wide and treats 'Police Officer' and 'Police Officer III' as orthogonal; these encoders give a low-dimensional (d~30) representation in which morphologically similar strings get similar vectors, capturing the latent structure of dirty categories with thousands of unique values. MinHash needs no vocabulary at all (handles unbounded/streaming cardinality and unseen categories natively); Gamma-Poisson learns d interpretable latent categories that summarize the long tail.
scales_to_many_features: Per-column the cost is bounded: output is d (=30) dims regardless of cardinality, so adding more high-cardinality string columns adds only d dims each — far cheaper than one-hot. MinHash is fully stateless and embarrassingly parallel / streaming-friendly (no fit, O(#ngrams) per string). Gamma-Poisson is fit online so it also scales to large row counts. The paper does not test a 70+-feature joint pipeline; evidence is per-column encoder quality, not many-feature interaction.
downstream_model: Primarily gradient-boosted trees (XGBoost) for the benchmark; also tested with other supervised learners. The encoders are model-agnostic dense featurizers, not an end-to-end deep model.
pretraining_objective: MinHash: none (stateless hashing, no learning). Gamma-Poisson: unsupervised generative factorization of n-gram counts (Poisson likelihood, Gamma prior) fit online — target-agnostic, so no label leakage unlike target encoding.
key_results: Across multiple real-world dirty-categorical datasets (employee salaries, traffic violations, medical charges, open-payments, road-safety, etc., 17+ tasks), the substring encoders match or beat one-hot, target/impact encoding, fastText, and tf-idf bag-of-n-grams when the categorical column is high-cardinality and dirty. MinHash gives near-best accuracy at the lowest cost and with zero fitting; Gamma-Poisson matches similarity-encoding accuracy while being far more scalable and adding interpretability (each latent dim reads as a substring-topic). Similarity encoding is strong on accuracy but does not scale (needs prototype set + pairwise string distances).
baselines_compared: one-hot encoding, target/impact (Bayesian) encoding, 3-gram similarity encoding (their prior work), tf-idf bag-of-n-grams, fastText embeddings, and a hashing baseline. Downstream learner held fixed (gradient boosting) to isolate the encoder.
transferable_idea: Encode a high-cardinality / open-vocabulary string column as a fixed-width (d~30) dense vector built from its CHARACTER n-grams rather than its identity — via stateless MinHash LSH (no vocabulary, handles unseen values, streaming) or Gamma-Poisson n-gram topic factorization (interpretable, label-free). This decouples the token width from cardinality and gives graceful OOV/cold-start behavior that a plain learned embedding table lacks.
limitations: Stated/inferred: (1) Pure categorical scope — no numerical/temporal/missing handling; it is a column encoder, not a full tokenizer. (2) Output is a dense real vector, not a discrete token / learned embedding-table entry, so plugging it into a transformer feature tokenizer needs an extra projection and loses end-to-end target-shaping. (3) MinHash dimensions are individually uninterpretable and not metric-meaningful coordinate-wise (only collision-rate ~ Jaccard is meaningful), so coordinate-wise use in non-tree models (MLP/attention) is weaker than in trees that split on inequalities. (4) Gamma-Poisson fit cost and topic-count d are hyperparameters; n-gram granularity (3-gram) is a fixed choice. (5) Captures lexical/morphological similarity, NOT semantic similarity ('NYC' vs 'New York City' only match if they share n-grams).
pitfalls: (1) The headline 'no fitting, works on streaming' is true ONLY for MinHash; Gamma-Poisson DOES fit (online EM) and is stateful — easy to conflate. (2) Benefit is conditional on the column being genuinely dirty/high-cardinality; on clean low-cardinality categoricals one-hot/target encoding ties or wins, so this is not a universal default. (3) Evaluated mainly with gradient-boosted trees, which exploit MinHash's inequality-splittable signature; the same MinHash vector fed to an attention/MLP tokenizer may underperform because coordinate magnitudes are arbitrary hash minima — do NOT assume the tree result transfers to a deep tokenizer unchanged. (4) Character-n-gram similarity is purely lexical; conflates strings that merely share substrings and misses synonyms.
relevance_to_topic: Directly fills the high-cardinality leg of a 70+-mixed-feature tokenizer: it is the canonical vocabulary-free, OOV-robust way to turn messy string columns (job titles, codes, free-text categoricals) into compact fixed-width dense tokens, complementing learned entity embeddings (which need a closed vocabulary) and target encoding (which leaks label).
code_available: yes — original methods are the dirty_cat MinHashEncoder and GapEncoder, now merged into skrub (https://skrub-data.org , https://github.com/skrub-data/skrub). Mature, maintained, scikit-learn-compatible transformers.
